title: Lower Bound

content.problemStatement:
You are given a sorted array numsnumsnums of length nnn and an integer xxx. 

Your task is to find the lower bound of xxx in the array.

The **lower bound** of `x` is defined as the **first (smallest) index** `i` such that `nums[i] >= x`.


If no such index exists, return `n` (the size of the array).
The array is sorted in **non-decreasing order**.
Function DeclarationFunction Namesolvesolvesolve – This function finds the lower bound index of a given value in a sorted array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A reference to a sorted array of integers.
xxx : The integer value whose lower bound is to be found.
Return Value
Returns the smallest index iii such that nums[i]≥xnums[i] \ge xnums[i]≥x
Returns nnn if no such index exists
Constraints
1≤n≤1051 \leq n \leq 10^51≤n≤105
−105≤nums[i],x≤105-10^5 \leq nums[i], x \leq 10^5−105≤nums[i],x≤105
numsnumsnums is sorted in **non-decreasing (ascending) order**

Input Format

One line containing two integers: nnn and xxx
One line containing nnn space-separated integers — the sorted array


Output Format

Print a single integer — the lower bound index
constraints: nums is sorted in non‑decreasing order
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers n and x. The second line contains n space‑separated integers representing the sorted array in non‑decreasing order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer – the smallest 0‑based index i such that nums[i] ≥ x. If no such index exists, print n.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first index with value ≥ 7 is index 3 (0‑based) because nums[3] = 8.
All elements are ≥ -1, so the smallest index is 0.
The value 5 appears at index 4, which is the first position where nums[i] ≥ 5.

solution.approach: Because the array is sorted, the lower bound can be found with binary search. Maintain a search window [low, high) where low is the smallest possible answer and high is one past the last index. At each step compute mid. If nums[mid] < x, the answer must be to the right of mid, so set low = mid + 1; otherwise the answer is at mid or to the left, so set high = mid. When low == high the loop ends and low is the first index with value ≥ x, or n if such an element does not exist. This runs in O(log n) time per test case and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log n) per test case, O(T · log n) total

tcGeneratorPython:
def generate_case(i, RINT, POWER):
    # Example generator for the hidden test cases above
    if i == 1:
        print(1)
        print(1, -100000)
        print(-100000)
    elif i == 2:
        print(1)
        print(1, 0)
        print(0)
    # Add similar branches for i=3..20 as needed
